name: Chemosh
description: Chemosh was the national god of the Moabites, a people who lived in the land east of the Dead Sea, roughly corresponding to modern-day Jordan. He was considered a powerful and fearsome deity, often associated with war, destruction, and divine wrath. Archaeological evidence, particularly the Mesha Stele, provides the primary source of information about Chemosh and the Moabite religion. The stele, erected by King Mesha of Moab in the 9th century BCE, details Mesha's victories over Israel, which he attributed to Chemosh's favor and power. The stele also describes the dedication of captured cities and people to Chemosh, including the practice of herem, a form of ritual destruction. While the exact nature of the sacrifices offered to Chemosh is debated, biblical texts accuse the Moabites of child sacrifice, a claim supported by some interpretations of the Mesha Stele and archaeological findings in the region. Chemosh was believed to protect his people and grant them victory in battle, but he demanded strict adherence to his laws and rituals, and his wrath was to be feared. He was a central figure in Moabite identity and culture, representing their strength, independence, and connection to their land.
subtitle: National God of the Moabites

keyMyths.title: Mesha's Victory
keyMyths.description: King Mesha attributes his victory over Israel to Chemosh's power and favor, detailing the reconquest of Moabite lands.

keyMyths.title: Dedication to Chemosh
keyMyths.description: Captured cities and people are dedicated to Chemosh through herem, a form of ritual destruction and sacrifice.

keyMyths.title: Wrath of Chemosh
keyMyths.description: Moabites believed that displeasing Chemosh would result in devastating consequences, including military defeat and natural disasters.
keyMyths.source: Biblical Texts and Interpretations of the Mesha Stele

sources.author: André Lemaire
sources.work: Studies in the Mesha Inscription and Moab
sources.description: Analysis of the Mesha Stele and its historical context.
sources.text: Analysis of the Mesha Stele and its historical context.
